title: Tokenization
lesson:
A language model never actually reads letters or words. Before any text reaches the model, it must be chopped into small pieces called tokens (the little chunks a model treats as one unit), and each piece is swapped for a number. That whole chopping-and-numbering step is tokenization.

If Tokens are the bricks, tokenization is the machine that makes the bricks. Get this wrong and everything downstream wobbles.

Why not just use words or letters?

Think of a chef prepping vegetables. You could chop everything into one giant block (whole words), but then a word the chef has never seen, like a brand-new username, has no chunk for it. Or you could mince everything into tiny dust (single letters), so nothing is ever unknown, but now even short sentences become hundreds of pieces and the model has to work much harder.

Tokenization picks the middle ground: chop into subwords (word fragments, like token + ization). Common words stay whole; rare words break into familiar parts. The chef gets pieces big enough to be efficient, but small enough that nothing is ever truly unknown.

How the chopping is learned: BPE

The most common method in 2026 is Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) (a recipe for building a vocabulary by repeatedly merging the most common pair of pieces). Here is the second analogy. Imagine you are inventing shorthand for a busy office. You start with single letters. You notice t and h appear together constantly, so you invent one symbol for th. Then th and e show up together a lot, so you merge those into the.

You keep merging the most frequent pairs until you have a fixed-size dictionary, maybe roughly 100,000 to 200,000 entries in modern models. That learned dictionary is the vocabulary, and the rules for splitting text are the tokenizer.

Crucially, modern BPE starts from raw bytes (the smallest unit of computer data, a number 0–255), not letters. This is why it never truly fails: any emoji, any language, any weird symbol can always be expressed as bytes, so there is always some valid split. The trade-off: text far from the training data, like rare languages or long code identifiers, gets chopped into many small pieces and costs more.

Why does the dictionary size matter so much? It is a balancing act:

  • A bigger vocabulary packs more text into each token, so prompts get shorter and cheaper — but the model needs more memory to store every entry.
  • A smaller vocabulary is leaner, but makes the same text longer.

Model builders tune this deliberately, which is one reason newer models often squeeze a sentence into fewer tokens than older ones.

Encode and decode

Tokenization runs both directions. Encoding turns your text into a list of token IDs (the numbers) to feed the model. Decoding turns the model's output IDs back into text you can read. Let us watch it happen with tiktoken, the tokenizer OpenAI ships:

import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("o200k_base")  # ~200k vocab
ids = enc.encode("Tokenization is sneaky!")
print(ids)            # e.g. [2748, 2065, 382, 70751, 0]
print(len(ids))       # 5 tokens
print([enc.decode([i]) for i in ids])
# ['Token', 'ization', ' is', ' sneaky', '!']

Notice the leading spaces: ' is' includes its space. Tokenizers usually attach the space to the front of the next word, which is why "hello" and " hello" can be different tokens. That tiny detail trips up a lot of beginners.

Why this matters in real products

Every API bills you per token, not per word, so tokenization directly drives your Cost-per-Token Analysis. It also sets how much fits in the Context Windows.

A rough rule for English: 1 token is about 4 characters, or roughly ¾ of a word, so 1,000 tokens is around 750 words. But that ratio collapses for code, JSON, or non-English text, which can be far more token-hungry.

Different model families use different tokenizers, so the same sentence is a different token count on each. You cannot mix them. Once text is tokens and IDs, the model maps each ID to an Embeddings vector and the real thinking begins, which is the start of How AI Models Work.

💡 Always count tokens with the exact tokenizer of the model you are calling. Estimating with a different one will quietly break your cost and length math.
mentalModel: Tokenization is a chef's prep station that chops any text into reusable subword chunks and labels each chunk with a number. The model only ever sees the labels, never the words.
takeaways: Tokenization splits text into subword chunks, then swaps each for a number.
BPE learns the split by merging frequent byte pairs, so nothing is unknown.
Token counts drive cost and context limits — and vary by tokenizer and language.

diagram.svg: <svg class="dg" viewBox="0 0 720 340" role="img" aria-label="How tokenization turns text into subword chunks and token IDs"><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="12" markerHeight="12" refX="9" refY="5" orient="auto"><path class="dg-arrowhead" d="M0,0 L11,5 L0,10 z"/></marker></defs><text class="dg-title" x="360" y="34" text-anchor="middle">From raw text to token IDs</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="190" y1="110" x2="250" y2="110" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><line class="dg-edge" x1="470" y1="110" x2="530" y2="110" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box" x="50" y="80" width="140" height="60" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="120" y="108" text-anchor="middle">Raw text</text><text class="dg-sub" x="120" y="128" text-anchor="middle">"Tokenization"</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-accent" x="250" y="80" width="220" height="60" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="360" y="108" text-anchor="middle">Subword tokens (BPE)</text><text class="dg-sub" x="360" y="128" text-anchor="middle">"Token" + "ization"</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-good" x="530" y="80" width="140" height="60" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="600" y="108" text-anchor="middle">Token IDs</text><text class="dg-sub" x="600" y="128" text-anchor="middle">[2748, 2065]</text><text class="dg-title" x="360" y="210" text-anchor="middle">More vocabulary entries = fewer tokens</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-2" x="70" y="240" width="260" height="70" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="200" y="268" text-anchor="middle">Small vocab</text><text class="dg-sub" x="200" y="290" text-anchor="middle">"un"+"believ"+"able" = 3 tokens</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-2" x="390" y="240" width="260" height="70" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="520" y="268" text-anchor="middle">Large vocab</text><text class="dg-sub" x="520" y="290" text-anchor="middle">"unbelievable" = 1 token</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Text is split into subword tokens, then mapped to numeric IDs; a larger vocabulary packs the same word into fewer tokens.

mistakes: Assuming 1 token equals 1 word. It is roughly ¾ of a word for English, but code, JSON, and other languages can be far more token-heavy. Always measure, never guess.
Counting tokens with the wrong tokenizer. Each model family splits text differently, so a sentence that is 10 tokens on one model may be 14 on another. Use the model's own tokenizer.
Forgetting the leading space. "hello" and " hello" are often different tokens, so naive string concatenation can change token counts and even outputs.
Trusting character or word counts for limits. The Context Windows limit is in tokens, not characters, so a 'short' chunk of dense code can blow past the limit unexpectedly.
Ignoring special tokens. Markers like a start-of-text or end-of-text token are added automatically and count toward your total; forgetting them causes off-by-a-few budgeting errors.
Expecting reversible, clean splits. Tokens are not syllables or letters, so a model struggles to count letters or reverse a word reliably, because it never sees individual characters.
exercise:
Install tiktoken (pip install tiktoken) and write a tiny script that encodes three strings with the o200k_base encoding: an English sentence, the same sentence translated into another language (or a chunk of JSON), and a long made-up word like antidisestablishmentarianism. For each, print the token count and the decoded pieces. Notice which inputs cost the most tokens and why.

Stretch goal: Take one paragraph and tokenize it with two different encodings (for example cl100k_base versus o200k_base). Compare the token counts and explain, in one sentence each, where they split differently and what that means for your API bill.
solution: Hint: the non-English text and the JSON almost always produce more tokens than plain English of the same length, because they sit further from what the tokenizer was trained to merge. The made-up word splits into several subword pieces (like anti, dis, establish...) instead of one. For the stretch goal, o200k_base (a newer, larger vocabulary) usually packs common English into fewer tokens than cl100k_base, which means lower cost for the same text. Use enc.decode([single_id]) in a loop to see each piece, and watch for the leading spaces glued to the front of words.
summary: Tokenization chops text into subword tokens and turns each into a number the model can use. Modern models learn the split with Byte-Pair Encoding over raw bytes, so nothing is ever unknown. Token counts drive cost and context limits, vary by tokenizer and language, and never line up neatly with words or letters.

keyTerms.def: The process of chopping text into tokens and converting each one into a number before the model sees it.
One small chunk of text the model treats as a single unit, often a whole word or a word fragment.
A piece of a word (like 'ization'), used so common words stay whole while rare words break into familiar parts.
The popular method that builds a vocabulary by repeatedly merging the most frequent pair of pieces, starting from raw bytes.
The fixed dictionary of all tokens a tokenizer knows, often roughly 100,000–200,000 entries in modern models.
The integer label assigned to each token; the model works with these numbers, not the text itself.
Encoding turns text into token IDs to feed the model; decoding turns the model's output IDs back into readable text.
A reserved marker (like start-of-text or end-of-text) added automatically that still counts toward your token total.

quiz.q: Why do modern tokenizers split text into subwords instead of whole words or single letters?
options: Subwords keep common words efficient while still handling rare or unseen words
Subwords let the model read individual letters more accurately
Whole-word tokenizers are illegal under current licensing rules
Single-letter splitting makes prompts cheaper than any other method
quiz.explain: Subwords are the middle ground: frequent words stay as one token (efficient), and rare words break into familiar fragments so nothing is truly unknown. Single letters would balloon the token count, not lower it.

quiz.q: Modern BPE tokenizers start from raw bytes rather than letters. What is the main benefit?
options: It makes every word exactly one token long
Any emoji, language, or symbol can always be expressed, so nothing is ever unhandled
It removes the need for a vocabulary entirely
It guarantees the same token count across all models
quiz.explain: Because all text reduces to bytes (0–255), there is always some valid split for any input. It does not make every word one token, and each model still has its own vocabulary and counts.

quiz.q: Your API call is rejected for exceeding the context limit, even though your text 'looks short'. The text is dense JSON. What is the most likely cause?
options: JSON is not supported by tokenizers
You measured length in characters or words, but JSON is token-hungry and uses far more tokens
The model counts whitespace as two tokens each
JSON always doubles the character count before sending
quiz.explain: Limits are measured in tokens, not characters or words. Dense JSON, code, and non-English text sit far from the tokenizer's merges, so they explode into many tokens for relatively little visible text.

quiz.q: Switching from an older tokenizer to one with a much larger vocabulary, what happens to the same English sentence?
options: It uses more tokens because each entry stores more data
It uses roughly the same number of tokens since vocabulary size is irrelevant
It tends to use fewer tokens because common chunks fit in single entries
It can no longer be decoded back into readable text
quiz.explain: A larger vocabulary can store longer common chunks as single tokens, so the same text usually packs into fewer tokens (often cheaper). The trade-off is more model memory to hold the bigger dictionary, not a higher token count.
